example_background: ByteDance, creator of Douyin (TikTok's Chinese version) and other content platforms, delivers short-video, news, and entertainment services to natural persons in China. The services rely on user-generated content, viewing habits, device information, and preferences to personalize feeds and recommendations. With millions of daily active users within the territory, ByteDance manages large-scale data processing to enhance user engagement and content distribution.
process_and_outcome: ByteDance established a dedicated Personal Information Protection Committee and conducted platform-wide audits. It implemented data localization, user consent tools, and transparent privacy policies, gaining regulatory recognition for proactive compliance measures without facing enforcement actions.
source_rule: (1) for the purpose of providing products or services to natural persons within the territory;

example_background: Meituan provides food delivery, travel booking, and local services to natural persons in China through its app. The services require collecting user locations, order details, contact information, and consumption patterns to match users with merchants and drivers. Operating nationwide, Meituan processes data for on-demand services used by individuals in urban and rural areas alike.
process_and_outcome: Authorities investigated and fined Meituan RMB 3.22 billion for improper data use in algorithmic recommendations. The company was mandated to overhaul its data governance, delete unnecessary data, and implement stricter access controls.
source_rule: (1) for the purpose of providing products or services to natural persons within the territory;

example_background: Pinduoduo operates a group-buying e-commerce platform serving natural persons across China with discounted goods and social shopping features. It gathers user social networks, purchase intents, device IDs, and location data to enable group deals and promotions targeted at individual consumers within the territory.
process_and_outcome: After early regulatory scrutiny, Pinduoduo invested in compliance infrastructure including privacy-by-design principles and regular third-party audits. It avoided major penalties and was cited for improved data practices in subsequent reviews.
source_rule: (1) for the purpose of providing products or services to natural persons within the territory;

example_background: A Shanghai-based online education platform provided tutoring and course services to natural persons in China, collecting student profiles, learning progress, parental contacts, and video interaction data. The platform used this information to customize lesson plans and marketing for users within multiple provinces.
process_and_outcome: The local Cyberspace Administration investigated and imposed a RMB 1.2 billion fine for unauthorized cross-border data transfers and excessive collection. The platform ceased operations pending full compliance rectification.
source_rule: (1) for the purpose of providing products or services to natural persons within the territory;

example_background: China Mobile, a state-owned telecommunications provider, delivers mobile communication, internet, and value-added services to natural persons throughout China. Services include voice calls, data plans, and apps that process subscriber identities, call records, location data, and usage patterns for billing and network optimization.
process_and_outcome: China Mobile rolled out a nationwide data compliance program with user data dashboards and consent management systems. Regulators commended the initiative, allowing seamless service continuation without disruptions.
source_rule: (1) for the purpose of providing products or services to natural persons within the territory;
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: Didi Global Inc., operator of China's largest ride-hailing platform, collected extensive user data including location, travel patterns, and personal identifiers from millions of users within China. Following its New York Stock Exchange listing in June 2021, authorities scrutinized how Didi analyzed user conduct through data processing to optimize services and expand market presence. The platform's algorithms assessed individual user behaviors to personalize offerings and predict demand.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China initiated a cybersecurity review in July 2021, finding illegal collection and processing of personal information. Didi was ordered to remove its apps from domestic app stores, fined 1.2 billion yuan in July 2022, and required to conduct data audits and rectification measures.
source_rule: (2) to analyze and assess the conduct of natural persons within the territory;
relevant_rules: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

example_background: ByteDance, parent of Douyin and TikTok, processes vast amounts of user-generated content and behavioral data from users in China. In response to regulatory scrutiny, it established internal mechanisms to assess user conduct for content moderation, data minimization, and consent verification, aligning operations with national data protection standards.
process_and_outcome: After a 2022 joint inspection by regulators, ByteDance cooperated fully, receiving administrative guidance rather than penalties. It enhanced data classification, user consent processes, and conduct analysis protocols, demonstrating ongoing adherence through annual compliance reports.
source_rule: (2) to analyze and assess the conduct of natural persons within the territory;

example_background: JD.com, a major e-commerce platform, utilized seller data to analyze purchasing and listing behaviors, entering 'choose one of two' exclusive arrangements that restricted sellers from using rival platforms. This was examined during a broader antitrust crackdown on internet platforms in China.
process_and_outcome: SAMR's 2021 investigation led to JD.com pledging voluntary rectification, avoiding fines by terminating exclusive agreements and submitting compliance commitments. The platform implemented self-assessment mechanisms for seller conduct monitoring.
source_rule: (2) to analyze and assess the conduct of natural persons within the territory;

example_background: Pinduoduo collected and analyzed user shopping habits, social sharing behaviors, and location data extensively. Regulators identified issues in how the platform processed this data for targeted marketing and user profiling without adequate consent mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: CAC and SAMR conducted a 2021 review, resulting in a 1.35 billion yuan fine in January 2024 for illegal data use. Pinduoduo was required to overhaul its data analysis practices and conduct regular internal audits.
source_rule: (2) to analyze and assess the conduct of natural persons within the territory;

example_background: China Telecom, a state-owned telecommunications giant, implemented a comprehensive data governance framework to analyze customer usage patterns, call records, and online activities for service optimization while ensuring data localization and security. This involved structured processes for assessing individual conduct in compliance with national laws.
process_and_outcome: Following regulatory audits in 2022, China Telecom received approval for its practices, with no penalties imposed. It continues to report quarterly on data handling and user conduct assessments as part of ongoing supervision.
source_rule: (2) to analyze and assess the conduct of natural persons within the territory;
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: Yuanfudao, an online education provider, used student performance data and learning behavior analytics to customize courses. During the 2021 crackdown on after-school tutoring, regulators examined its data processing practices for compliance with restrictions on analyzing minor students' conduct.
process_and_outcome: After halting profit-oriented tutoring and restructuring, Yuanfudao aligned with regulations, receiving operational approval in 2022 without fines. It adopted privacy-by-design in conduct analysis tools.
source_rule: (2) to analyze and assess the conduct of natural persons within the territory;

example_background: Shanghai Didiglobal Technology Co., Ltd., a subsidiary linked to Didi, handled customer service data for ride-hailing users, including phone numbers, addresses, and service records. The company stored data of 599 million users without obtaining explicit consent for the collection and processing methods used. Data was retained longer than required for business purposes, and access controls were insufficient, allowing employees unnecessary access to personal details. This occurred amid broader regulatory focus on tech giants' data practices in 2021-2022.
process_and_outcome: CAC conducted an administrative penalty, imposing a fine of 1.18 billion yuan on the subsidiary. The company was required to cease illegal data processing, delete unlawfully collected data, and conduct internal audits to improve compliance. Additional orders included public announcements of the violations.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_background: Alibaba, through its Cainiao logistics arm, collected facial recognition data and personal information from merchants and users without clear consent. The practices involved mandatory biometric data submission for platform access, exceeding minimal necessity principles. Data was processed for user authentication but lacked transparency in policies. This was part of a 2022 enforcement wave targeting large internet platforms handling vast amounts of personal data from e-commerce activities.
process_and_outcome: Hangzhou authorities fined Alibaba's Cainiao 100,000 yuan for mishandling facial data. The company rectified its data collection forms, added consent mechanisms, and updated privacy policies to specify data use purposes.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of and Handling of Illegal Collections and Use of Personal Information by Apps

example_background: Shuidichou, a Beijing-based online fundraising platform, required users to provide phone numbers, ID numbers, and bank details for account registration and donations. The platform collected data without informing users of processing purposes and retained it without justification. Over 100 million pieces of personal information were involved, shared with third-party advertisers without authorization. Operations continued from 2018 until regulatory action in 2022.
process_and_outcome: Beijing police fined the platform and its personnel 50.1 million yuan. The company shut down operations, deleted non-compliant data, and the case was publicized as a warning to similar platforms.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_background: JD.com's e-commerce app sought permissions for camera, microphone, and location access without linking them to specific service functions or obtaining separate consents. User data including purchase histories and preferences were processed extensively, impacting hundreds of millions of shoppers.
process_and_outcome: Under CAC supervision, JD.com was ordered to rectify within 20 days in 2022, adding granular consent prompts and privacy policy disclosures. No direct fine but part of broader enforcement.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_background: Baidu's app collected device information, WiFi lists, and personal details without privacy policy notifications during user registration. The search engine giant processed data for advertising personalization affecting over a billion users.
process_and_outcome: CAC fined Baidu 44 million yuan in November 2022, requiring immediate app store removal and fixes before reinstatement.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_background: Chongqing Chuntianyouwo, operator of a mini-program on WeChat, gathered phone numbers and other data from users without consent during service sign-ups. The program facilitated various online services reaching thousands of users.
process_and_outcome: Fined 100,000 yuan by local authorities in 2022, with orders to halt data collection and notify affected users.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_name: China Telecom Shanghai Customer Data Exposure
example_background: A Shanghai branch of China Telecom exposed personal data of 192 million mobile subscribers, including names, phone numbers, and service details, due to improper database configurations accessible online from 2019.
process_and_outcome: Shanghai CAC investigated in 2022, fining China Telecom Shanghai 101 million yuan. The company secured the database, informed users, and enhanced security training.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_name: Ant Group Data Security Review Compliance
example_background: Ant Group, affiliate of Alibaba, underwent a comprehensive cybersecurity review post its halted IPO in 2021. The company holds financial data of hundreds of millions including transaction records, credit scores, and behavioral data used in lending algorithms.
process_and_outcome: CAC approved Ant's data security rectification plan in 2023 after implementing isolated data processing, consent-based sharing, and regular audits, allowing continued operations under supervision.
source_rule: Article 1: This Law is drafted on the basis of the Constitution to protect rights and interests in personal information, to regulate activities handling personal information, and to promote the reasonable use of personal information.

example_background: TikTok, operated by ByteDance, faced scrutiny over its handling of personal information from Chinese users through its global platform. The app collects user data including location, device information, and behavioral patterns from individuals within mainland China, with processing occurring on servers located overseas in regions such as Singapore and the United States. This setup raised questions about compliance with requirements for cross-border data transfers, as the platform's international operations involve transferring such data outside the PRC without fully adhering to specified approval or assessment procedures mandated for protecting data of PRC residents.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) conducted an investigation in 2023, focusing on TikTok's data practices. ByteDance implemented remedial measures including enhanced data localization, security assessments, and filing for cross-border transfer approvals. No public fine was imposed, but the company committed to ongoing audits and compliance enhancements.
source_rule: This law is also applicable to activities outside the mainland PRC [“overseas“--ed.] that handle the personal information of natural persons within the territory of the PRC, in any of the following circumstances:
relevant_rules: Measures for Security Assessment of Cross-Border Data Transfers

example_background: Alibaba Group, through its cloud computing arm Alibaba Cloud, processes personal data of Chinese customers for e-commerce, logistics, and cloud services. Some data handling occurs via international data centers in Southeast Asia and Europe, involving information from PRC residents such as purchase records and user profiles. In response to evolving regulations, Alibaba established mechanisms to conduct security assessments for outbound transfers.
process_and_outcome: Alibaba filed its first cross-border data transfer security assessment with CAC in 2023, demonstrating adherence through standard contracts and impact assessments. The filing was accepted, allowing continued operations without penalties.
source_rule: This law is also applicable to activities outside the mainland PRC [“overseas“--ed.] that handle the personal information of natural persons within the territory of the PRC, in any of the following circumstances:
relevant_rules: Measures for Security Assessment of Cross-Border Data Transfers

example_background: Tencent's WeChat platform serves over 1 billion users, many in mainland China, collecting data like chat logs, payment details, and social connections. Portions of this data are processed on servers in locations such as Singapore and the U.S. for global service delivery and backups, subjecting it to overseas handling rules for PRC residents' information.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, Tencent completed CAC security assessments for cross-border transfers and implemented data localization strategies. No enforcement actions were taken, with the company receiving approval for compliant transfers.
source_rule: This law is also applicable to activities outside the mainland PRC [“overseas“--ed.] that handle the personal information of natural persons within the territory of the PRC, in any of the following circumstances:
relevant_rules: Provisions on Standard Contracts for Cross-Border Transfer of Personal Information

example_background: A Shanghai police database containing personal information of over 1 billion Chinese citizens, including names, addresses, IDs, and criminal records, was exposed online via an unsecured ElasticSearch server accessible from overseas. The data handling extended beyond mainland PRC, allowing foreign access without safeguards.
process_and_outcome: Discovered by researchers in 2022, the incident led to the database being secured. No formal fine announced, but it prompted national discussions on data security, with local authorities enhancing protections.
source_rule: This law is also applicable to activities outside the mainland PRC [“overseas“--ed.] that handle the personal information of natural persons within the territory of the PRC, in any of the following circumstances:

example_background: China Telecom, a state-owned telecom giant, handles vast amounts of user data from PRC subscribers, including call records, internet usage, and location data. For international roaming and cloud services, some processing occurs on overseas infrastructure in Asia-Pacific regions.
process_and_outcome: China Telecom obtained CAC approval for cross-border transfers in 2023 via security assessments, maintaining operations without disruptions or penalties.
source_rule: This law is also applicable to activities outside the mainland PRC [“overseas“--ed.] that handle the personal information of natural persons within the territory of the PRC, in any of the following circumstances:
