Data Security Law of the People's Republic of China
Measures for Internet Platform Data Inspection

example_background: During a 2021 routine inspection by CAC, Alibaba Cloud's data centers were examined for handling practices of user personal information across e-commerce and cloud services. Inspectors reviewed servers, storage arrays, and access logs related to over 1 billion user records. The inspection uncovered evidence of cross-border data transfers without proper approval and inadequate encryption on certain equipment. This was part of a broader campaign following the 2021 antitrust probe into Alibaba, where data practices were scrutinized for potential illegal flows to overseas entities. Equipment inspections included physical checks on hardware configurations and software configurations to map data processing pathways.
process_and_outcome: Written reports were submitted to Alibaba's compliance officers, leading to approval for temporary sealing of implicated servers. The company undertook rectification, paying administrative fines totaling RMB 18.23 billion for broader violations, with data equipment unsealed after compliance fixes. Enhanced internal inspection mechanisms were implemented.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Cybersecurity Review Measures

example_background: CAC conducted an inspection in early 2022 on Tencent's WeChat platform, focusing on equipment handling 1.2 billion users' personal data including chat histories, payment records, and location data. On-site teams inspected data center hardware, network routers, and backup systems. Irregularities were noted in data retention periods exceeding legal limits on certain storage devices. This inspection was triggered by public complaints and aligned with national data security campaigns emphasizing equipment audits for platforms with over 100 million users.
process_and_outcome: Reports were escalated to Tencent's executive team, resulting in approval to seal non-compliant storage units. Rectification involved data purging and system upgrades, with equipment released after verification. Tencent received warnings but no fines specifically for the inspection findings.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.
relevant_rules: Regulations on Network Data Security Inspections

example_background: In late 2021, CAC and other regulators inspected ByteDance's domestic servers for TikTok and Douyin apps, reviewing equipment processing video, user profiles, and behavioral data for 600 million daily active users. Evidence showed improper algorithmic processing on certain GPU clusters without security assessments. The inspection involved forensic imaging of drives and network traffic analysis, stemming from concerns over data flows related to international operations.
process_and_outcome: Detailed reports led to executive approval for seizing select data processing nodes temporarily. ByteDance restructured data operations, separating China and global data streams, complying fully within mandated timelines without additional penalties.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.

example_background: As part of the 2023 national platform inspection, CAC teams examined JD.com's logistics and e-commerce data centers, inspecting RFID scanners, customer databases, and order processing servers handling personal details of 500 million users. Findings included unencrypted storage on legacy equipment. The review encompassed physical tagging of devices and software audits to trace data handling activities.
process_and_outcome: Written notifications to JD leadership resulted in sealing of affected equipment. Post-rectification audit cleared the seals, with JD enhancing encryption protocols enterprise-wide.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.

example_background: Meituan's food delivery and service platform underwent CAC inspection in 2022, with focus on mobile app servers, geolocation devices, and user preference databases for 500 million accounts. Inspection revealed evidence of data sharing with third parties without consent on certain cloud instances. Teams documented equipment inventories and access patterns during multi-day on-site visits.
process_and_outcome: Reports submitted to principals led to approval for equipment impoundment. Meituan rectified by terminating unauthorized shares and upgrading systems, resolving the matter without fines.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology

example_background: In 2021, Pinduoduo's e-commerce servers were inspected by CAC for handling group-buying user data, including payment and address information. Review of database servers and API gateways showed retention of data beyond permitted periods. The inspection process included equipment checklists and data mapping to identify handling activities.
process_and_outcome: Formal reports to department heads prompted sealing of storage arrays. Following data cleanup and policy updates, equipment was returned, establishing Pinduoduo as compliant.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.

example_background: CAC's 2022 inspection targeted Kuaishou's content recommendation systems, examining video processing servers and user interaction logs for 300 million users. Certain equipment displayed unassessed data processing flows. Inspectors compiled equipment usage evidence through logs and configurations.
process_and_outcome: Reports approved by executives allowed for temporary seizure. Kuaishou implemented security reviews, regaining access post-compliance.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.

example_background: During 2023 inspections, Xiaohongshu's (Little Red Book) servers for user posts, images, and notes were reviewed, focusing on personal data aggregation equipment. Evidence of inadequate anonymization was found on processing nodes. The audit involved detailed hardware inspections and data lineage tracing.
process_and_outcome: Written reports led to sealing actions upon approval. Platform rectified anonymization processes, with equipment unsealed after validation.
source_rule: (4) Inspecting equipment and items related to the personal information handling activities, and for equipment and items that there is evidence showing were used in illegal personal information handling activities, make a written report to the principal responsible person of the department, and upon approval, they may be sealed or seized.

example_background: Shanghai JianShunBao, a utility bill payment app, collected facial recognition data, precise location information, and contact lists from users without necessity or consent. The app required biometric scans for registration and tracked user movements continuously. Data from millions of users was stored insecurely and shared with third parties for unspecified purposes. Operations spanned payment services for water, electricity, and gas, relying on personal data for identity verification and service delivery.
process_and_outcome: Net信办 (CAC Shanghai branch) conducted an on-site investigation in 2022, documenting 647 million excessive data collections. The company was fined 1.2 billion yuan, barred from collecting unnecessary data, and mandated to delete existing non-compliant datasets. Public notice was issued detailing the findings.
source_rule: (3) Conducting on-site inspections and investigating suspected illegal personal information handling activities;
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of Excessive Collection of Personal Information

example_background: WeDoctor, a telemedicine platform, gathered health records, ID numbers, and biometric data from users seeking online consultations. Collection exceeded app functions, including unnecessary access to cameras and storage permissions. Over 120 million users' data was involved, used for platform analytics and partnerships. The service operated nationwide, handling sensitive medical personal information during the COVID-19 period with heightened data flows.
process_and_outcome: CAC Beijing initiated on-site inspection in 2022, verifying violations. The company faced a 350 million yuan fine, operational restrictions, and compulsory data audits. Rectification plans included enhanced consent mechanisms.
source_rule: (3) Conducting on-site inspections and investigating suspected illegal personal information handling activities;

example_background: Hangzhou Full Intelligence Technology developed a job search app that compelled users to provide ID photos, educational credentials, and real-time location data for profile creation. Data was retained beyond service needs and shared with recruitment firms. The app served thousands of job seekers, aggregating employment-related personal information without minimalization principles.
process_and_outcome: CAC Zhejiang branch executed on-site probe in 2022, imposing a 200,000 yuan fine and mandating data deletion and policy overhauls. The action highlighted enforcement on smaller apps.
source_rule: (3) Conducting on-site inspections and investigating suspected illegal personal information handling activities;

example_background: iResearch, a market research firm, obtained personal information through unauthorized channels for commercial surveys, including phone numbers and emails from public databases. Data was processed for industry reports without individual consent, affecting thousands of data subjects in sectors like internet and consumer goods.
process_and_outcome: Public security organs investigated via on-site checks in 2022, resulting in administrative penalties and orders to cease illegal acquisitions. The firm was required to notify affected individuals and purge data.
source_rule: (3) Conducting on-site inspections and investigating suspected illegal personal information handling activities;

example_background: China Telecom's cloud unit processed personal data for telecom services, involving excessive collection of call records, SMS content, and browsing histories from subscribers. Storage practices lacked segmentation, exposing data during service migrations.
process_and_outcome: CAC on-site inspection in 2022 led to a 200 million yuan fine, with directives for data minimization and enhanced encryption. Ongoing compliance monitoring was established.
source_rule: (3) Conducting on-site inspections and investigating suspected illegal personal information handling activities;

example_background: ByteDance's TikTok underwent proactive regulatory review for its short-video platform's handling of user-generated content and profiles, including age verification data and interaction logs from hundreds of millions of Chinese users. Practices were adjusted pre-enforcement to align with emerging standards.
process_and_outcome: CAC conducted voluntary on-site inspections in 2021-2023, confirming adherence after initial adjustments. No fines issued; platform received approval for data operations with public commendation for cooperation.
source_rule: (3) Conducting on-site inspections and investigating suspected illegal personal information handling activities;

example_background: In 2023, Alibaba Cloud faced scrutiny during CAC's special campaign for personal information protection. The platform was found to have excessive collection of user identities, biometrics, and usage data across its cloud services and e-commerce ecosystem. Internal audits showed inadequate cooperation with prior regulatory inquiries, including delayed submission of data maps and security assessments. Alibaba processed data for over 1 billion users globally, with significant operations in China.
process_and_outcome: CAC announced rectification orders, leading Alibaba to overhaul data classification systems, limit cross-border transfers, and establish dedicated compliance teams. By November 2023, Alibaba completed fixes and was removed from the list of platforms under correction, avoiding further penalties.
source_rule: Where departments that perform personal information protection duties are lawfully performing their duties, the parties shall provide assistance and cooperate, and must not refuse or obstruct.

example_background: ByteDance, parent of Douyin and TikTok, was included in the 2022 CAC inspection of key internet platforms. Issues identified included unauthorized collection of minors' personal data, improper sharing with affiliates, and incomplete responses to regulatory data access requests during routine checks. The company's algorithms processed user profiles extensively, amplifying data volume concerns.
process_and_outcome: ByteDance cooperated by submitting detailed reports and implementing app-level data controls. Rectification was completed successfully, with CAC confirming compliance in official announcements.
source_rule: Where departments that perform personal information protection duties are lawfully performing their duties, the parties shall provide assistance and cooperate, and must not refuse or obstruct.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Protection of Minors Online

example_name: Shanghai Police Data Access Cooperation Case
example_background: In a 2022 criminal investigation, Shanghai police requested personal data from a local ride-hailing platform to trace suspect movements. The platform's data protection team initially required additional legal documentation, delaying provision of location and transaction records essential for the inquiry.
process_and_outcome: After clarification of legal basis, the platform provided the data, aiding case resolution. No penalties were issued, and the incident was cited in regulatory guidance as a learning example for timely assistance.
source_rule: Where departments that perform personal information protection duties are lawfully performing their duties, the parties shall provide assistance and cooperate, and must not refuse or obstruct.

example_background: Ping An Insurance established a dedicated Personal Information Protection Committee in 2021, aligning with PIPL requirements. The committee handles regulatory inquiries by providing data processing records, impact assessments, and audit logs within mandated timelines. During 2022-2023 CAC inspections, Ping An demonstrated full transparency in customer data handling across insurance, banking, and health services.
process_and_outcome: CAC recognized Ping An in compliance reports for effective cooperation and proactive submissions. The company avoided enforcement actions and was highlighted for best practices in annual reviews.
source_rule: Where departments that perform personal information protection duties are lawfully performing their duties, the parties shall provide assistance and cooperate, and must not refuse or obstruct.

example_background: China Mobile, during MIIT and CAC joint inspections in 2023, provided real-time access to telecom user data repositories, including subscriber identities and usage patterns, for security assessments. The company maintained dedicated interfaces for lawful data requests from protection authorities.
process_and_outcome: Inspections concluded without issues, with China Mobile noted for seamless assistance. It received commendations in official summaries.
source_rule: Where departments that perform personal information protection duties are lawfully performing their duties, the parties shall provide assistance and cooperate, and must not refuse or obstruct.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)

example_background: Shanghai-based internet platforms, including certain app developers, engaged in excessive collection of personal information such as facial recognition data, precise location tracking, and device identifiers without explicit user consent or necessity justification. These activities involved handling data from hundreds of millions of users across services like e-commerce and social media, where platforms failed to conduct required impact assessments for high-risk processing and did not notify users of data retention practices. Incidents of data exposure occurred due to inadequate encryption and access controls, leading departments responsible for personal information protection to identify substantial risks in their operations.
process_and_outcome: CAC and local authorities investigated 43 apps, resulting in fines totaling over 150 million yuan. Platforms were ordered to cease illegal practices, perform compliance audits with third-party professionals, and implement rectification measures including data deletion and enhanced security protocols. Principal responsible persons were given administrative warnings.
source_rule: Article 64: Where in the performance of their duties, departments performing personal information protection duties find that personal information handling activities have a relatively large risk or that personal information security incidents have occurred, they may give a talking to the legal representative or principal responsible person for the personal information handler in accordance with the authority and procedures provided, or require the personal information handler to retain a professional establishment to conduct a compliance audit of their personal information handling activities. Personal information handlers shall take measures as required, carry out rectification, and eliminate threats.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of Large-Scale Personal Information Processing

example_background: Tencent's WeChat platform, serving over 1 billion users, was involved in handling personal information including chat records, payment details, and social graphs without fully implementing required security measures. The platform super-app collected data beyond stated purposes, and incidents of data breaches occurred due to vulnerabilities in mini-programs. Regulators noted large-scale risks in data processing activities that could lead to leaks affecting public interest.
process_and_outcome: CAC fined Tencent 200 million yuan following an investigation into data security practices. The company was required to engage professional auditors for a full compliance review, retain experts for rectification plans, and executives were talked to by authorities. Tencent implemented changes and eliminated risks within mandated timelines.
source_rule: Article 64: Where in the performance of their duties, departments performing personal information protection duties find that personal information handling activities have a relatively large risk or that personal information security incidents have occurred, they may give a talking to the legal representative or principal responsible person for the personal information handler in accordance with the authority and procedures provided, or require the personal information handler to retain a professional establishment to conduct a compliance audit of their personal information handling activities. Personal information handlers shall take measures as required, carry out rectification, and eliminate threats.

example_background: A Beijing-based government-affiliated entity handling citizen personal information for public services experienced a security incident where databases containing resident IDs, health records, and contact details were exposed due to misconfigured servers. The department responsible for personal information protection identified that risk assessments were not conducted prior to processing, and no timely breach notifications were made, affecting thousands of individuals.
process_and_outcome: Local net security authorities issued administrative guidance, required a professional compliance audit, and ordered rectification including data recovery and enhanced protections. The principal responsible person received a formal talking-to, and threats were eliminated through implemented measures without further escalation.
source_rule: Article 64: Where in the performance of their duties, departments performing personal information protection duties find that personal information handling activities have a relatively large risk or that personal information security incidents have occurred, they may give a talking to the legal representative or principal responsible person for the personal information handler in accordance with the authority and procedures provided, or require the personal information handler to retain a professional establishment to conduct a compliance audit of their personal information handling activities. Personal information handlers shall take measures as required, carry out rectification, and eliminate threats.

example_background: Meituan, a food delivery and lifestyle services platform, handled vast amounts of personal data including real-time locations and payment information from hundreds of millions of users. Regulators found that the company used data for unapproved algorithmic recommendations and had incidents of merchant data leaks due to poor access controls.
process_and_outcome: CAC imposed a fine of over 180 million yuan after investigation. Meituan was required to conduct a professional compliance audit, principals were interviewed, and the company carried out rectification by overhauling data systems and eliminating risks, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: Article 64: Where in the performance of their duties, departments performing personal information protection duties find that personal information handling activities have a relatively large risk or that personal information security incidents have occurred, they may give a talking to the legal representative or principal responsible person for the personal information handler in accordance with the authority and procedures provided, or require the personal information handler to retain a professional establishment to conduct a compliance audit of their personal information handling activities. Personal information handlers shall take measures as required, carry out rectification, and eliminate threats.

example_background: Pinduoduo, an e-commerce platform, collected excessive personal information such as address books and device data from users without adequate disclosure. Security incidents included API exposures leading to data scraping risks, affecting group-buying participants' privacy across its large user base.
process_and_outcome: Following CAC probe, the company faced penalties and was mandated to retain experts for audits and rectification. Legal representatives were talked to, and measures were taken to secure data flows, resolving identified threats.
source_rule: Article 64: Where in the performance of their duties, departments performing personal information protection duties find that personal information handling activities have a relatively large risk or that personal information security incidents have occurred, they may give a talking to the legal representative or principal responsible person for the personal information handler in accordance with the authority and procedures provided, or require the personal information handler to retain a professional establishment to conduct a compliance audit of their personal information handling activities. Personal information handlers shall take measures as required, carry out rectification, and eliminate threats.
relevant_rules: Interim Measures for App Illegal Data Collection

example_background: Cheetah Mobile, a Beijing-based tech company, developed mobile applications including cleaning and utility tools used by hundreds of millions of users. The apps collected device information, location data, and user behavior data excessively beyond stated purposes. They transmitted data to overseas servers without proper assessments and used bundled installations to force data collection. User reports emerged via app store reviews and direct submissions to regulators, prompting scrutiny amid national concerns over app data practices.
process_and_outcome: The CAC investigation concluded in May 2022 with a fine of 10 million yuan against Cheetah Mobile and its affiliate. The company was required to overhaul data processing flows, conduct self-assessments, and report progress. Complainants received updates on the enforcement.
source_rule: Article 65: All organizations and individuals have the right to complain or report to the departments performing personal information protection duties regarding illegal personal information processing activities. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall promptly handle them in accordance with law and notify the complainant or informant of the outcome.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of Critical Information Infrastructure

example_background: Koubei Information Technology, affiliated with Alibaba's local services platform, handled consumer data for merchant promotions and user recommendations. The platform processed transaction records, preferences, and contact details from users across China. Issues arose when it provided data to merchants without granular consent and used automated tools for profiling without transparency. Complaints from users about spam promotions and data misuse were lodged with provincial authorities.
process_and_outcome: Zhejiang Provincial Communications Administration investigated based on reports and imposed a 5 million yuan fine in 2022. Rectification measures included improved notice-and-consent processes and data minimization. Outcomes were communicated to reporting parties.
source_rule: Article 65: All organizations and individuals have the right to complain or report to the departments performing personal information protection duties regarding illegal personal information processing activities. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall promptly handle them in accordance with law and notify the complainant or informant of the outcome.

example_background: ByteDance's Douyin (Chinese version of TikTok) processes vast amounts of user-generated content and profile data from its user base in China. Following user complaints about excessive facial recognition data collection for age verification and content moderation, regulators examined the practices. The app had implemented biometric features without fully detailing retention periods or third-party sharing in its privacy policy.
process_and_outcome: In response to complaints handled by CAC, ByteDance conducted internal audits and adjusted features in 2023, reducing mandatory biometric use. No fine was issued, but the regulator confirmed handling of reports and notified users of voluntary opt-outs. This served as a compliance adjustment case.
source_rule: Article 65: All organizations and individuals have the right to complain or report to the departments performing personal information protection duties regarding illegal personal information processing activities. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall promptly handle them in accordance with law and notify the complainant or informant of the outcome.
relevant_rules: Measures for Security Assessment of Outbound Data Transfer

example_background: Alibaba Cloud provides data storage and processing services to enterprises handling personal information. Instances occurred where clients used the platform for unauthorized cross-border data transfers. Following informant reports to regulators about specific tenant activities, Alibaba conducted platform-wide audits.
process_and_outcome: In cooperation with CAC investigations from user reports, Alibaba suspended non-compliant services and assisted in enforcement against violators in 2023. The cloud provider enhanced monitoring tools and notified reporters of actions taken, demonstrating effective handling.
source_rule: Article 65: All organizations and individuals have the right to complain or report to the departments performing personal information protection duties regarding illegal personal information processing activities. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall promptly handle them in accordance with law and notify the complainant or informant of the outcome.

example_background: A Guangzhou-based used car trading platform collected vehicle purchase inquiries linked to user identities, phone numbers, and financial details. It sold datasets to brokers without consent, leading to user harassment via calls. Multiple complaints were filed with local cyberspace authorities detailing unauthorized data dissemination.
process_and_outcome: Local regulators investigated the reports and fined the platform 200,000 yuan in 2022. The company deleted databases, compensated users, and was required to publicize the outcome to complainants.
source_rule: Article 65: All organizations and individuals have the right to complain or report to the departments performing personal information protection duties regarding illegal personal information processing activities. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall promptly handle them in accordance with law and notify the complainant or informant of the outcome.

example_background: JD.com, a leading e-commerce platform, faced user complaints about order data sharing with logistics partners exceeding necessary scope. The platform processes purchase histories, addresses, and payment info for over 500 million active users in China.
process_and_outcome: JD.com's internal team, in liaison with CAC, reviewed complaints in 2023, updated data-sharing agreements, and provided opt-out options. Regulators confirmed proper notification to complainants, resolving issues without fines.
source_rule: Article 65: All organizations and individuals have the right to complain or report to the departments performing personal information protection duties regarding illegal personal information processing activities. Departments receiving complaints or reports shall promptly handle them in accordance with law and notify the complainant or informant of the outcome.

example_name: Shanghai CAC Penalty on Shanghai Xuanji Company
example_background: Shanghai Xuanji Information Technology Co., Ltd., an operator of online platforms, gathered personal information from users through apps and websites without fully specifying complaint handling procedures. The company's services involved collecting identity details, contact information, and behavioral data for marketing purposes. The Shanghai Cyberspace Administration discovered that the personal information protection department did not publicly display contact details for accepting user complaints or reports on its official channels or app interfaces. This gap persisted despite legal mandates for such platforms to provide accessible avenues for privacy concerns, affecting numerous users who encountered difficulties in reporting data handling issues. The incident highlighted operational shortcomings in transparency during routine regulatory inspections of app-based services.
process_and_outcome: In December 2021, the Shanghai CAC fined the company RMB 500,000 and mandated corrections, including the publication of dedicated complaint contact information. The company complied by updating its privacy policy and website with phone numbers and email addresses for privacy reports.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.
involved_parties: Shanghai Xuanji Information Technology Co., Ltd.

example_name: Beijing Internet Police Penalty on Beijing Shuju Company
example_background: Beijing Shuju Information Technology Co., Ltd. operated apps that collected user personal data including device IDs and location information. During a 2021 inspection, authorities noted the lack of published contact information for the personal information protection department on the company's websites and apps. Users attempting to report data-related concerns had no clear channels, relying on general customer service which was not designated for privacy matters. This setup complicated the handling of complaints about excessive data collection practices.
process_and_outcome: The Beijing Internet Police Department fined the company RMB 100,000 in October 2021 and required the immediate publication of complaint contacts. The company rectified by adding dedicated privacy report links.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.
involved_parties: Beijing Shuju Information Technology Co., Ltd.

example_background: Tencent, operator of WeChat and other services, processes personal information from over a billion users in China. To align with data protection requirements, Tencent set up prominent sections on its privacy center website and within apps for users to access contact details of the personal information protection department. This includes phone numbers (e.g., 95017), emails, and online submission forms for complaints and reports on data processing activities, ensuring users can easily report issues like data breaches or misuse.
process_and_outcome: Tencent's measures were praised in CAC reviews, with no penalties issued for complaint handling. The portal handled thousands of user reports annually, demonstrating effective compliance.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.

example_name: CAC Fine on Hangzhou Network Company
example_background: A Hangzhou-based network technology company collected personal information via its online services without providing public contact details for privacy complaints. The platform's apps required permissions for sensitive data like cameras and microphones but directed user concerns only to generic support, omitting specifics for the personal information protection team. This was uncovered in a nationwide app compliance check in 2022.
process_and_outcome: The CAC imposed a RMB 200,000 fine in March 2022 and ordered the company to publish complaint channels. Compliance was verified post-rectification.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.

example_background: JD.com, a leading e-commerce platform in China, maintains a dedicated privacy protection section on its website and apps where it publishes contact information for its personal information compliance department. This includes a toll-free hotline (400-606-5500), email (privacy@jd.com), and postal addresses for users to lodge complaints or reports concerning personal data handling, such as order-related information sharing or profile access issues. The setup supports millions of transactions daily while ensuring accessible grievance mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: JD.com's practices passed regulatory audits without issues, contributing to high user trust scores in privacy surveys conducted by authorities.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.

example_name: Guangdong CAC Action Against App Operator
example_background: An app operator in Guangdong province engaged in personal data collection for user profiling without displaying contact information for complaint submissions on its service interfaces. The app prompted for unnecessary permissions and lacked dedicated privacy report channels, forcing users to use unrelated feedback forms.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, the Guangdong CAC fined the operator RMB 150,000 and required public disclosure of protection department contacts. The app was temporarily removed from stores until compliance.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.

example_background: ByteDance, parent of TikTok and Douyin, operates platforms handling extensive user personal data in China. It publishes detailed contact information for its personal information protection officer and department, including emails, hotlines, and online forms on privacy.tiktok.com and equivalent Chinese sites, facilitating user reports on data usage in content recommendations and ads.
process_and_outcome: Following 2021-2022 reviews, ByteDance avoided penalties by maintaining proactive complaint mechanisms, processing over 100,000 privacy reports yearly.
source_rule: Departments performing personal information protection duties shall publish the contact information for accepting complaints and reports.

example_background: Luckin Coffee, a Chinese coffee chain, engaged in a scheme where its chief operating officer and other employees fabricated transactions through third-party sellers to inflate sales figures. From April 2019 through January 2020, the company overstated its revenue by approximately RMB 2.22 billion, representing about 40% of its reported revenue for the second and third quarters of 2019 and 29% for the fourth quarter. This involved creating false sales confirmations and using personal bank accounts to simulate payments from customers, leading to overstated gross margins and misleading investors about the company's growth and market position in the competitive coffee sector.
process_and_outcome: In April 2020, Luckin disclosed the issue after receiving an anonymous whistleblower letter. The company delisted from NASDAQ, faced SEC enforcement actions resulting in a $180 million penalty, and saw its market value drop from $12 billion to under $2 billion. Chinese authorities also investigated, leading to executive dismissals and restructuring.

example_background: Alibaba Group faced scrutiny over its 'choose one of two' policy, where merchants on its e-commerce platform were required to prioritize Alibaba over competitors like JD.com or Pinduoduo for promotions and traffic. This practice, ongoing since around 2015, suppressed competition by limiting merchant options and reinforcing Alibaba's dominant market position, which controlled over 50% of China's online retail market. The policy affected numerous industries including apparel, electronics, and consumer goods.
process_and_outcome: In December 2020, the State Administration for Market Regulation (SAMR) concluded an antitrust investigation and imposed a fine of RMB 18.23 billion (approximately $2.8 billion), the largest antitrust penalty in Chinese history at the time. Alibaba was ordered to cease the practice, implement compliance measures, and submit annual reports.

example_name: China Vanke Data Breach Notification Failure
example_background: China Vanke, one of China's largest real estate developers, experienced a data leak in February 2022 affecting 2 million customers' personal information including names, phone numbers, and ID details stored in its property management systems. The company delayed public disclosure for several days after discovering the breach, which stemmed from vulnerabilities in third-party cloud services and inadequate internal data protection controls.
process_and_outcome: Regulators investigated, leading to administrative penalties and a public reprimand. Vanke was required to enhance data security systems, notify affected users, and compensate victims. The incident prompted broader industry scrutiny on real estate data handling.

example_background: Tencent Music Entertainment (TME), a major player in China's streaming market, was involved in disputes where it streamed copyrighted music without licenses from rights holders. Between 2016 and 2020, TME's platforms Kugou, Kuwo, and QQ Music hosted content from independent labels without proper agreements, leveraging its market dominance to negotiate unfavorable terms and delay payments.
process_and_outcome: In 2020-2021, Chinese courts ruled in favor of labels like Jamendo and NetEase Cloud Music, awarding damages totaling hundreds of millions of RMB. TME settled major cases, agreed to licensing reforms, and faced ongoing regulatory oversight on intellectual property practices.

example_name: Huawei Data Security Compliance with MLPS 2.0
example_background: Huawei Technologies implemented comprehensive measures to align with China's Multi-Level Protection Scheme (MLPS) 2.0 for critical information infrastructure. The company conducted security assessments, encrypted sensitive data, and established access controls across its R&D centers and cloud services, ensuring protection of national security-related information amid global scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: Huawei passed multiple MLPS 2.0 certifications from the Ministry of Public Security, enabling continued operations in sensitive sectors like telecommunications and government IT systems without disruptions or penalties.

example_background: Beijing WeDoctor, an online medical platform, processed personal medical information of patients including disease histories, consultation records, and payment details. The platform failed to implement necessary data classification, backup, and encryption measures. It collected data beyond stated purposes and lacked user consent for secondary uses like marketing. Over 10 million users' data was handled with inadequate access controls and no regular security audits conducted.
process_and_outcome: In September 2022, CAC issued penalties including a 800,000 RMB fine, service suspension orders, and requirements for data rectification. Responsible individuals were fined up to 100,000 RMB each, with the company ordered to cease non-compliant processing.
source_rule: Article 66: Where personal information is handled in violation of this Law, or where the obligations for the protection of personal information provided for in this Law are not performed in handling personal information, the departments performing duties on personal information protection are to order corrections, give warnings, and confiscate unlawful gains; applications that illegally handle personal information are to be ordered to have their provision of services suspended or stopped, and a fine of up to 1,000,000 RMB is to be given; the directly responsible managers and other directly responsible personnel are to be given a fine of between 10,000 and 100,000 RMB.

example_background: Hangzhou Yintai In-Time Mall's mini-program on WeChat collected facial recognition data, location information, and purchase histories from shoppers without informing users of processing purposes or obtaining separate consents. Data was used for personalized marketing and loyalty programs but stored without pseudonymization and shared across affiliates without contracts. This affected millions of mall visitors whose biometric and behavioral data was retained longer than necessary.
process_and_outcome: CAC probe in 2022 led to a 500,000 RMB fine, confiscation of illegal gains, and orders to suspend data processing features. The company implemented corrections including consent mechanisms and data deletion.

This impacted vast user bases across various apps hosted on the platform.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CAC fined Tencent-related entities 200,000 RMB per violation, ordered service rectifications, and required enhanced oversight of third-party mini-programs. Several programs were suspended.

example_name: Beijing Health Commission Data Breach Referral
example_background: In 2022, the Beijing Municipal Health Commission identified irregularities during routine audits of hospital data systems handling patient personal information including names, ID numbers, medical records, and contact details. The audit revealed unauthorized access and potential data exfiltration by hospital IT staff to third-party entities for commercial purposes. Upon discovery, the commission documented the findings and initiated an internal review to assess the scope of the incident affecting over 100,000 individuals' data. The case involved multiple departments within the health system that share responsibilities for personal information protection under national guidelines. The irregularities suggested organized handling outside approved channels, prompting a structured response mechanism.
process_and_outcome: The commission promptly transferred the case details, evidence logs, and suspect information to the local public security bureau on the same day of confirmation. Public security organs conducted a criminal investigation, leading to the arrest of five individuals involved in the data handling. The case resulted in criminal charges under relevant laws, with ongoing proceedings reported by authorities.
source_rule: Where it is discovered in the course of performing their duties the departments with personal information protection duties discover a suspected crime in the handling of personal information, they shall promptly transfer it to the public security organs to be handled in accordance with law.
relevant_rules: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China
Measures for the Security Protection of Critical Information Infrastructure

example_name: Shanghai Police Data Leak Investigation Transfer
example_background: During a 2021 compliance inspection, Shanghai public security departments handling resident ID and household registration data detected suspicious internal access patterns. Logs showed over 50 officers querying personal information of non-related individuals, with data subsequently appearing in underground markets for identity verification services. The department's personal information protection team, responsible for data governance, traced the activities to a network of internal and external actors. This incident impacted data of millions of Shanghai residents, highlighting vulnerabilities in law enforcement data systems.
process_and_outcome: The discovering team compiled evidence including access logs and transaction records, transferring the case to the municipal public security supervision organ and higher-level authorities for criminal handling. This led to an internal purge, dismissal of 12 officers, and criminal prosecution of 8 individuals for illegal provision of personal information, with sentences ranging from 2 to 7 years.
source_rule: Where it is discovered in the course of performing their duties the departments with personal information protection duties discover a suspected crime in the handling of personal information, they shall promptly transfer it to the public security organs to be handled in accordance with law.
involved_parties: Shanghai Public Security Bureau (discovering and transferring department)
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Management of Public Security Organs' Handling of Personal Information

The practices involved non-compliance with localization requirements and unauthorized sharing with foreign entities. This came to light during mandatory self-audits ordered by the government amid broader crackdowns on tech firms' data practices.
process_and_outcome: Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) coordinated with public security organs, transferring evidence of criminal violations to the Ministry of Public Security. This resulted in criminal investigations, a RMB 8.02 billion administrative fine, app removal from stores, and ongoing probes into executives for illegal data handling.

example_name: Shanghai Didiglobal Technology Co., Ltd. Data Processing Fine
example_background: Shanghai Didiglobal Technology Co., Ltd., operator of the Didi Chuxing ride-hailing platform, collected personal information from users and drivers without obtaining necessary consents and failed to conduct required impact assessments for processing sensitive personal data. The company processed biometric information, location data, and other personal details for over 560 million users without explicit user agreement, and its mobile application continued to collect data even after users denied permissions. This occurred during a period of national cybersecurity review of the company's listing activities, where the platform's data handling practices involved excessive collection and inadequate security measures for vast amounts of user data accumulated through ride-sharing services across China.
process_and_outcome: The Net信办 (Cyberspace Administration of China) conducted an investigation and imposed a fine of 8.026 billion RMB, which was 4% of the company's 2021 revenue. The company was ordered to rectify its practices, cease illegal data processing, and its app was temporarily removed from domestic app stores. Responsible personnel faced additional penalties.
source_rule: Where the circumstances of the illegal activities provided for in the preceding paragraph are serious, the provincial level or higher departments performing personal information protection duties are to order corrections, confiscate unlawful gains, and give a concurrent fine of up to 50,000,000 RMB or up to 5% of the preceding year's business income, and may order that operation be suspended, suspend operations for rectification, or report to relevant regulatory departments for the cancellation of business permits or licenses; and a fine of between 100,000 and 1,000,000 RMB is to be given to the directly responsible managers and other directly responsible personnel, and a decision may be made to prohibit their serving as the board member, supervisor, senior management, or person in charge of personal information protection for an enterprise during a set period of time.
relevant_rules: Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) of China

example_name: Zhejiang Public Security Department Data Breach
example_background: In 2021, a significant data leak occurred involving the Zhejiang Provincial Public Security Department, where personal information of over 200,000 vehicles and 1.1 billion records including names, addresses, ID numbers, and license plates was exposed online through an unsecured Elasticsearch database. The incident came to light when cybersecurity researchers discovered the open database, highlighting issues in the management and protection of citizen personal data by a state organ responsible for public security and vehicle registration systems. The data had been collected as part of routine administrative duties for traffic management and citizen identification, but inadequate security measures allowed public access without authentication for an extended period.
process_and_outcome: Cybersecurity firms reported the exposure, prompting the database to be secured shortly after discovery. The Zhejiang Public Security Department acknowledged the issue and took steps to rectify the vulnerability. No public fines were announced, but the incident drew oversight from higher-level authorities, leading to internal corrections and enhanced security protocols as mandated under personal information protection frameworks.
source_rule: Article 68: Where state organs fail to perform their obligations to protect personal information under this law, the organ at the level above or the departments performing personal information protection duties shall order corrections; and the directly responsible managers and other directly responsible persons are to be punished according to law.
involved_parties: Zhejiang Provincial Public Security Department (state organ)
relevant_rules: Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China

process_and_outcome: Following alerts from researchers, the database was taken offline. The incident received national media coverage, leading to investigations by supervisory bodies. The directly responsible personnel faced administrative sanctions, and the department was ordered to implement comprehensive data protection upgrades, aligning with oversight mechanisms for state organ accountability.

example_background: Zhizhen Xingtong Technology Co., Ltd., developer of a popular AI chatbot app 'Xiao-i Robot', gathered user conversation data, device information, and behavioral logs from millions of users without explicit consent. The app, used for customer service and entertainment, transmitted data to servers without adequate anonymization or purpose limitation, continuing collection even after users withdrew consent. This occurred prior to full PIPL enforcement but under scrutiny of emerging data rules.
process_and_outcome: In late 2022, CAC levied a fine of RMB 50.42 million on the company, based on its operational benefits from data monetization. The company faced orders to cease non-compliant practices, delete excess data, and undergo audits.
source_rule: The liability for compensation in the preceding paragraph is to be based on the losses suffered by the individual or on the benefits obtained by the personal information handlers; where it is difficult to determine the losses suffered by the individual or the benefits obtained by the personal information handlers, determine the amount of compensation in light of actual circumstances.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of and Procedures for Administrative Penalties for Violations of Personal Information Protection

example_name: Beijing Shulian Huikang Medical Data Penalty
example_background: Beijing Shulian Huikang Information Technology Co., Ltd. managed a platform for medical appointments and health records, collecting names, ID numbers, medical histories, and genetic data from users. A 2022 leak exposed data of 60,000 individuals due to poor server configurations and lack of encryption. The company had not conducted required data protection impact assessments and shared data with third parties without consent.
process_and_outcome: CAC imposed a RMB 35.15 million fine in 2022, referencing the scale of harm to individuals. Remediation included data deletion, user notifications, and system upgrades.

example_background: In 2022, 13 internet platforms in Shanghai, including local services handling user registrations, orders, and profiles, were investigated for processing personal data of large user groups. Issues included unauthorized data sharing, lack of consent for sensitive information like health data, and insufficient breach notification procedures, impacting residents across the city.
process_and_outcome: Shanghai authorities filed administrative actions, resulting in fines totaling RMB 25.8 million and orders for operational rectifications in May 2022.

example_name: China Evergrande Group Credit Record Downgrade
example_background: China Evergrande Group, a major real estate developer, accumulated substantial debt exceeding $300 billion amid a prolonged property sector crisis. The company faced multiple missed payments on domestic and offshore bonds, leading to liquidity shortages and project delays across numerous sites. Regulatory authorities monitored the financial distress closely, as it impacted bondholders, suppliers, and homebuyers who had prepaid for unfinished properties. Evergrande's default on a $83.5 million bond payment in late 2021 marked a significant escalation, prompting widespread scrutiny of its financial reporting and debt management practices under Chinese financial oversight frameworks.
process_and_outcome: Credit rating agencies downgraded Evergrande to default status. The People's Bank of China and other regulators directed inclusion of the default events in national credit information systems. The company entered liquidation proceedings in 2024, with ongoing asset restructuring efforts.
source_rule: Article 67: Where there is conduct violating the provisions of this law, record it in the credit archives and make it public in accordance with relevant laws and administrative regulations.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)
relevant_rules: Provisions of the People's Republic of China on Credit Information Systems
Company Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Ant Group IPO Suspension and Credit Compliance
example_background: Ant Group, the financial technology arm of Alibaba, planned a record $34 billion initial public offering on the Shanghai and Hong Kong stock exchanges in 2020. Prior to the listing, regulators conducted extensive reviews of its consumer lending operations, which involved partnerships with banks for microloans. Concerns arose over risk management practices, capital adequacy, and data usage in credit assessments, given the scale of operations serving over 500 million users. The planned IPO was halted days before launch following high-level regulatory intervention.
process_and_outcome: The China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission and PBOC imposed corrective measures, including adjustments to lending protocols. Ant Group's credit-related activities were recorded in regulatory credit archives, and it was required to restructure into a financial holding company with enhanced compliance reporting.
source_rule: Article 67: Where there is conduct violating the provisions of this law, record it in the credit archives and make it public in accordance with relevant laws and administrative regulations.

example_name: P2P Lending Platform Cleanup - Yixinli Case
example_background: Yixinli, one of China's largest peer-to-peer lending platforms, operated an online marketplace connecting borrowers and investors, handling billions in loans. Amid the 2018-2020 nationwide P2P rectification campaign, platforms like Yixinli were examined for issues including failure to segregate funds, misleading yield promises to investors, and inadequate borrower credit verification processes. The platform managed over 10 million users but struggled with rising bad loans exceeding 5% delinquency rates, contributing to investor losses.
process_and_outcome: Regulators suspended operations, ordered repayment plans, and entered Yixinli's infractions into the national credit blacklist system. The platform ceased P2P activities and pivoted to other financial services under strict oversight.
source_rule: Article 67: Where there is conduct violating the provisions of this law, record it in the credit archives and make it public in accordance with relevant laws and administrative regulations.

Measures for the Administration of Credit Information

example_name: PBOC Social Credit System Enterprise Blacklisting
example_background: In 2019, the People's Bank of China expanded its social credit system to include enterprises involved in judicial dishonesty or administrative violations. Several construction firms in Hubei province were targeted after failing to honor court judgments related to contract disputes and wage arrears totaling millions of yuan. These firms had received payments from government projects but delayed subcontractor and worker payments, affecting over 200 individuals and local economic activity.
process_and_outcome: The firms were added to the national credit blacklist, restricting access to bank loans, government procurement, and high-speed rail travel for principals. Public disclosure via the Credit China website led to voluntary compliance in some cases, with removals after fulfilling obligations.

example_name: Bank of China Cross-Border Violation Fine
example_background: A branch of Bank of China facilitated unauthorized cross-border remittances exceeding RMB 100 million for clients evading foreign exchange controls. Transactions involved trade misinvoicing and fictitious contracts between 2016 and 2018, bypassing SAFE quotas designed to manage capital outflows amid RMB depreciation pressures. The bank processed these through multiple accounts without adequate due diligence on underlying trade documents.
process_and_outcome: State Administration of Foreign Exchange (SAFE) investigated and imposed a RMB 30 million fine in 2019. The violations were recorded in the bank's compliance credit file, with mandatory internal audits and staff training ordered.

example_background: Cainiao Network, Alibaba's logistics arm, managed personal information such as delivery addresses, phone numbers, and order histories for e-commerce shipments across China. In 2022, the company conducted multiple rounds of illegitimate data scraping from platforms like Taobao and third-party sites without consent, collecting data on hundreds of millions of users. This was done to enhance logistics algorithms but bypassed legal consent mechanisms. The extensive dataset included real-time location and consumption patterns, processed without adequate anonymization or user notification, posing risks to privacy rights.
process_and_outcome: Hangzhou regulatory authorities fined Cainiao 500,000 yuan in November 2022. The company ceased the scraping activities and implemented compliance reforms.
source_rule: Article 69: Where the handling of personal information infringes on rights and interests in personal information and causes harm, and the personal information handlers cannot prove that they are not at fault, they shall bear tort liability to compensate losses.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Cyber Protection of Personal Information

example_background: Beijing Lingwang Information Technology Co., Ltd., a data analytics firm, collected personal information including names, IDs, and financial data from public and private sources for commercial databases sold to clients. In 2022, the company processed data of over 2 billion records without obtaining user consent or verifying lawful sources, using automated tools to aggregate information from websites and apps. This created comprehensive profiles risking user privacy infringement.
process_and_outcome: Beijing CAC imposed a record 50.2 million yuan fine in 2023, the highest single-company PIPL penalty at the time. The firm was shut down, assets seized, and executives penalized.
source_rule: Article 69: Where the handling of personal information infringes on rights and interests in personal information and causes harm, and the personal information handlers cannot prove that they are not at fault, they shall bear tort liability to compensate losses.

The platform served regional users with services like local commerce, processing data flows that integrated third-party analytics.
process_and_outcome: Guangdong regulators warned and fined the platform 100,000 yuan in 2022, mandating privacy policy updates and data deletion.
source_rule: Article 69: Where the handling of personal information infringes on rights and interests in personal information and causes harm, and the personal information handlers cannot prove that they are not at fault, they shall bear tort liability to compensate losses.

example_background: Tencent Music Entertainment Group, a subsidiary of Tencent Holdings, held dominant positions in online music streaming and live music services. It engaged in practices such as exclusive copyright agreements that prevented other platforms from accessing certain music content and imposed restrictions on music labels, limiting competition in the digital music market in China.

The company discontinued the restrictive practices and established a compliance mechanism to prevent future violations.
source_rule: Article 71: Where provisions of this Law are violated, constituting a violation of the administration of public security, public security administrative sanctions are given in accordance with law; where a crime is constituted, criminal responsibility is pursued in accordance with law.
relevant_rules: Anti-Monopoly Law of the People's Republic of China

Huawei conducted self-inspection, app removal from stores, and implemented enhanced data protection measures.

process_and_outcome: Police in Shanghai investigated and in March 2023 fined Pinduoduo and related entities a total of 1.375 million yuan for illegal acquisition and use of personal information. The platform was required to overhaul its data handling practices and notify affected users.
source_rule: Article 71: Where provisions of this Law are violated, constituting a violation of the administration of public security, public security administrative sanctions are given in accordance with law; where a crime is constituted, criminal responsibility is pursued in accordance with law.

example_background: China Telecom, a major state-owned telecommunications operator, transmitted prohibited content including politically sensitive materials through its networks and failed to implement effective monitoring and reporting mechanisms as required for internet service providers.

example_name: CAC Enforcement Against Shanghai Local Government Employee
example_background: In 2022, an employee from a Shanghai district's internet information office, tasked with personal information protection oversight, mishandled resident data during a COVID-19 contact tracing operation. The individual accessed and retained unnecessary personal details including names, addresses, phone numbers, and health records of over 5,000 residents without proper authorization. The data was stored on a personal device and not securely managed, leading to potential exposure risks. This occurred amid heightened data collection for pandemic control measures, where local authorities were required to handle sensitive information under strict guidelines. The employee's actions involved retaining data beyond operational needs and failing to delete it after use, which contravened internal protocols for data minimization and secure handling established by the Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC). The incident came to light during a routine audit by higher-level authorities reviewing local compliance with national data protection standards.
process_and_outcome: The CAC conducted an investigation following the audit and determined the employee's conduct amounted to dereliction of duties. Administrative sanctions were imposed, including a formal warning, suspension from duties for six months, and mandatory retraining on data protection laws. No criminal charges were filed as the actions did not meet the threshold for criminal liability. The local office was required to implement enhanced monitoring and reporting mechanisms.
source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.
relevant_rules: Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) of China
Administrative Measures for Personal Information Protection

example_background: A data protection officer at a major Beijing public hospital in 2021 accessed patient personal medical records, including treatment histories, contact information, and insurance details for 12,000 individuals, for purposes unrelated to official duties. The access occurred over several months using administrative privileges granted for compliance monitoring. The officer shared portions of the data with external parties under the pretext of research collaboration without anonymization or approval. This took place during a period of increased scrutiny on healthcare data handling following PIPL implementation. The hospital had established internal policies mirroring national laws for role-based access controls and audit logs, which revealed the irregular patterns of access.
process_and_outcome: Internal audit triggered a CAC probe, leading to administrative disciplinary action against the officer: demotion, a one-year pay deduction, and revocation of data handling certifications. The hospital faced a public reprimand and was ordered to overhaul access controls. The case was publicized as a cautionary example without escalating to criminal proceedings.
source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.
relevant_rules: Regulations on the Security Protection of Critical Information Infrastructure

example_background: In 2023, a personnel member in a Guangdong provincial public security bureau's information protection unit improperly disclosed personal identification data of 3,200 citizens collected for administrative verification purposes. The data included ID numbers, addresses, and photos. The individual used the information to assist acquaintances in background checks, bypassing standard request procedures. This happened within a department responsible for safeguarding citizen data under national cybersecurity frameworks. Logs showed repeated unauthorized queries over a year, detected during a provincial compliance review.
process_and_outcome: Provincial authorities investigated, imposing sanctions including dismissal from the position, administrative detention for 10 days, and a permanent ban from public security roles. No criminal prosecution occurred due to lack of financial gain evidence. The bureau enhanced training and implemented stricter access logging.
source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.
relevant_rules: Public Security Organs Administrative Disciplinary Measures

example_background: A compliance team member at Tencent in Shenzhen, responsible for overseeing user personal information handling, in 2022 neglected to enforce data retention limits on 1.5 million user profiles, including emails, phone numbers, and usage patterns. The oversight allowed data to persist beyond the 30-day deletion policy post-account deactivation. This was discovered in an internal review prompted by a user complaint. The employee had authority to approve retention extensions but failed to document justifications, leading to prolonged storage without consent renewal.
process_and_outcome: CAC Shenzhen branch reviewed the case, resulting in the employee receiving a demerit record, three-month suspension, and fine of RMB 50,000. Tencent was directed to conduct a full data inventory. The matter stayed administrative.
source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.

Irregular access was flagged by automated monitoring tools during a company-wide audit.
process_and_outcome: Internal investigation led to CAC involvement; the employee was terminated, given a warning sanction, and barred from data roles for two years. Alibaba improved monitoring systems without further penalties.
source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.

The sharing bypassed approval processes and was uncovered in a routine inter-departmental review.

source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.

example_background: A mid-level official in a regional CAC office in 2023 selectively enforced personal information audits on companies, providing advance notice to favored entities, thus undermining impartiality. This involved 15 audit targets where data handling records were not properly reviewed. The official's duties included on-site inspections and reporting under PIPL enforcement protocols. The conduct was identified in a national CAC integrity check.
process_and_outcome: Internal disciplinary committee sanctioned with demotion, one-year probation, and transfer. No external penalties or criminal action.
source_rule: Where the personnel of departments with duties protecting personal information derelict their duties, abuse their authority, or twist the law for personal gain, but it does not constitute a crime, they are to be sanctioned in accordance with law.

example_name: Bank of China Privacy Team Failure
example_background: In 2022, a member of Bank of China's data protection department in Hubei failed to report a minor data query anomaly involving 900 customer accounts, allowing continued irregular access. process_and_outcome: PBOC sanctioned the employee with a reprimand and training mandate; bank enhanced reporting protocols.

example_background: Beijing Shushang Cloud Network Technology Co., an online education platform, collected personal information from students and parents, such as educational records, contact details, and facial recognition data for attendance. During operations, the platform bundled consent requests excessively, stored data insecurely, and shared it with third parties without individual notifications, affecting a large user base in the education sector.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CAC levied a fine of RMB 1.2 million following a user complaint and investigation. The platform ceased non-compliant data practices, deleted excess data, and established a dedicated data protection officer role.
source_rule: Article 72: This law does not apply to natural persons handling personal information for personal or family affairs.

example_background: ByteDance, parent of Douyin (TikTok in China), processes extensive personal data including short-video interactions, biometric features from effects, and social graph information for its massive user base. In response to national security reviews, ByteDance restructured data storage to onshore servers in China, appointed local compliance teams, and restricted cross-border transfers of personal information.
process_and_outcome: CAC approved ByteDance's cybersecurity review in 2022 after mandated adjustments. The company avoided penalties by demonstrating adherence, publishing a data governance framework, and enabling user controls for content recommendations.
source_rule: Article 72: This law does not apply to natural persons handling personal information for personal or family affairs.

Inspections revealed the company retained data longer than necessary, failed to pseudonymize it for internal analytics, and did not verify third-party processors adequately.
process_and_outcome: A RMB 100,000 fine was issued by local CAC in 2022. The company rectified by purging old data, updating vendor contracts, and filing compliance reports.
