example_name: Beijing Health Commission COVID-19 Data Handling Case
example_background: During the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the Beijing Health Commission collected extensive personal health information from residents for epidemic tracking and statistical analysis. The data included names, ID numbers, phone numbers, travel history, and health status of millions of individuals. This information was processed for statistical purposes to generate reports on infection rates, mobility patterns, and resource allocation, which were archived by government health departments for long-term public health management and policy evaluation. The handling followed designated government protocols for data aggregation and de-identification in statistical outputs, ensuring that individual records were used solely for official statistical and archival needs amid the national emergency response.
process_and_outcome: The National Computer Network Emergency Response Technical Team/Coordination Center of China (CNCERT/CC) reviewed the practices and confirmed adherence to government-specified procedures. No enforcement actions were taken, and the data handling was upheld as a model for public health data management during crises.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.
relevant_rules: Personal Information Protection Law of the People's Republic of China (PIPL)
Cybersecurity Law of the People's Republic of China
Provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives by people's governments

example_name: Shanghai Public Security Bureau Data Breach Investigation
example_background: In 2022, a contractor working with the Shanghai Public Security Bureau improperly accessed and leaked personal information collected for administrative statistics, including resident registration data with names, addresses, and ID numbers intended for city planning and population statistics. The data was part of government-organized archival management for demographic analysis, but the contractor shared portions online without authorization, affecting thousands of records.
process_and_outcome: CAC and local authorities investigated the incident, leading to administrative penalties against the contractor and internal audits at the bureau. Fines totaling RMB 1.2 million were imposed, and enhanced data access controls were mandated.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.
relevant_rules: Provisions on statistical and archival personal information handling by governments

example_name: National Bureau of Statistics Census Data Processing
example_background: The Seventh National Population Census in 2020 involved the National Bureau of Statistics collecting personal information from over 1.4 billion residents, including age, ethnicity, education, employment, and household details. This data was handled exclusively for statistical compilation and national archival purposes under centralized government coordination, with strict separation of identifiable data from aggregated statistics released publicly.
process_and_outcome: Post-census audits by regulatory bodies verified compliance with government-approved protocols. The process was recognized for its scale and security, contributing to national planning without reported incidents.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.
relevant_rules: Statistics Law of the People's Republic of China

example_name: Guangdong Province Hospital Data Misuse Case
example_background: A public hospital in Guangdong Province, operating under provincial health department oversight, collected patient personal information for epidemiological statistics and disease outbreak archives in 2021. The data encompassed medical histories, contact details, and treatment records of over 50,000 individuals. An employee exported raw data for personal use, bypassing government-designated statistical aggregation processes.
process_and_outcome: Provincial regulators conducted an investigation, resulting in dismissal of the employee, a RMB 500,000 fine on the hospital, and mandatory retraining programs for data handlers.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.

example_background: Tianjin Municipal Archives Bureau implemented a digital archiving system in 2019 for historical administrative records containing personal information from government departments, such as birth, marriage, and residency records spanning decades. The system processed data strictly for archival preservation and statistical retrieval as per municipal government directives, using encryption and access logs to manage over 10 million digitized entries.
process_and_outcome: An independent audit by the Ministry of Culture and Tourism commended the system for aligning with national standards, with no compliance issues identified.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.

example_name: Zhejiang Education Department Student Data Incident
example_background: In 2023, the Zhejiang Provincial Education Department gathered student personal information for educational statistics and enrollment archives, including names, ages, academic performance, and family backgrounds from public schools province-wide. A vendor subcontracted for data entry sold subsets of the raw data to marketers, contravening the government's statistical processing protocols.
process_and_outcome: CAC investigated, imposing fines of RMB 2 million on the vendor and RMB 800,000 on the department, alongside requirements for data governance overhaul.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.

example_background: During the 2023 Economic Census, Shenzhen Statistics Bureau under the municipal government collected business owner personal information linked to enterprise data for statistical analysis and archival storage. Protocols included anonymization for public statistics while retaining identifiable records in secure government archives for longitudinal economic studies.
process_and_outcome: Regulatory review post-census confirmed full adherence, with the bureau receiving recognition for efficient and secure data management.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.

example_name: Hubei Public Security Identity Leakage Case
example_background: Hubei Province Public Security Department maintained a database of resident ID information for crime statistics and security archives in 2022. Due to inadequate segmentation, a departmental transfer of files exposed data to unauthorized internal users, involving over 100,000 records intended solely for official statistical and archival use.
process_and_outcome: Internal investigation led to disciplinary actions against staff, a RMB 1.5 million penalty, and system upgrades enforced by provincial regulators.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.

example_background: Chongqing Municipal Civil Affairs Bureau digitized marriage registration records containing personal details like names, dates, and addresses for statistical trends on family structures and archival preservation since 2021. The process adhered to city government guidelines, limiting access to aggregated stats while securing originals.
process_and_outcome: Audit by national archives administration validated the practices, noting effective controls and no incidents.
source_rule: Where the law has provisions on the handling of personal information for statistics and archives management activities organized and implemented by the people's governments at all levels and their relevant departments, those provisions are to be applied.

example_background: Tencent Holdings, operator of WeChat and various online services, proactively aligned its data practices with national definitions for network operators and critical infrastructure. Serving hundreds of millions of users, the company established comprehensive data classification systems, conducted regular security assessments, and ensured retention of necessary logs. In response to regulatory guidance, Tencent developed internal policies mirroring legal terms for personal information, network data, and cybersecurity events, integrating them into its platform operations across gaming, social media, and cloud services.
process_and_outcome: During 2022 CAC audits, Tencent passed with no major issues, receiving commendations for its data mapping and risk management frameworks. The company continued to expand compliance training and published annual transparency reports demonstrating adherence.

example_background: Didi Global, the ride-hailing giant, collected extensive user data including location, travel patterns, and personal identifiers from over 500 million users in China. Prior to its 2021 U.S. IPO, the company transferred sensitive data overseas without proper security assessments, contravening definitions of network data and cross-border transfer requirements. Regulators found that Didi's apps continued operations despite warnings, with data handling not fully compliant with operator obligations under cybersecurity frameworks.
process_and_outcome: CAC ordered Didi to cease new user registration in July 2021, followed by a 8.026 billion RMB fine in July 2022. The company underwent a full cybersecurity review, delisted from NYSE, and restructured data practices with government oversight.

The company conducted extensive testing on data encryption, log retention, and incident response for base stations and core networks deployed nationwide, ensuring alignment with national standards amid international scrutiny.
process_and_outcome: MIIT granted commercial 5G licenses in 2019 after rigorous compliance checks. Huawei maintained ongoing certifications through annual audits, supporting widespread 5G rollout without disruptions.

In 2023, inspections revealed improper storage and processing of user behavioral data, not fully meeting definitions for personal information controllers and security obligations, including inadequate anonymization and consent mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: CAC and Net信办 imposed a 50.05 million RMB fine in 2023 for violations in apps like Xigua Video. ByteDance rectified issues, enhanced data isolation, and submitted multi-year compliance plans.

The company's data classification and response mechanisms were examined, revealing gaps in applying definitions for network security events and personal data processors, affecting millions of customer records including orders and contacts.
process_and_outcome: CAC fined JD.com 10 million RMB in early 2022. The firm upgraded encryption, notified affected users, and conducted a full security overhaul under regulatory supervision.

Handling petabytes of user queries and location data, Baidu implemented classification schemes, retention policies, and third-party audits to match legal terms for operators and products.
process_and_outcome: CAC approved Baidu's AI models for public use in 2023 after compliance verification. The company released open-source compliance tools and received industry awards.

example_background: Beijing 58 Daojia, a platform providing home services, collected extensive personal data from users including names, phone numbers, addresses, and service records. In 2022, the company was found to have retained this data in identifiable form after users requested deletion, sharing it with affiliates without proper processing. The platform processed millions of user profiles without implementing measures to prevent re-identification, leading to data being accessible across its ecosystem. This occurred amid growing scrutiny on data handling practices in China's internet sector, where platforms aggregate vast amounts of personal information for matching services. The incident highlighted challenges in ensuring data cannot be linked back to individuals once collected for operational purposes.
process_and_outcome: CAC investigated and imposed a fine of RMB 30.85 million. The company was ordered to rectify its data processing practices, delete improperly retained data, and conduct internal audits. Public notice was issued detailing the findings.
source_rule: (4) "Anonymization" refers to the process in which personal information is handled so that it cannot be used to identify a specific natural person and cannot be restored after being so handled.

example_background: Shanghai Qiming Medical Technology Co., Ltd., an online health consultation platform, gathered personal health data such as medical histories, consultation records, and contact details from over 100,000 users. The company stored this data without applying techniques to render it non-identifiable, making it possible to trace back to specific individuals. In 2022, during a routine compliance check, it was discovered that backup databases retained raw data without pseudonymization or aggregation, exposing sensitive health information. This case underscored issues in the health tech sector where data is collected for AI-driven diagnostics and user matching but not adequately transformed.
process_and_outcome: CAC levied a fine of RMB 23.53 million. Remediation included data deletion, enhanced security protocols, and mandatory training. The enforcement action was part of a broader campaign targeting app compliance.
source_rule: (4) "Anonymization" refers to the process in which personal information is handled so that it cannot be used to identify a specific natural person and cannot be restored after being so handled.

example_background: Weibo Corporation, a social media platform, collected user posts, interactions, and profile data from over 500 million accounts. During a 2022 self-assessment prompted by regulatory notices, it identified datasets used for advertising where usernames and behaviors were not fully de-identified. The platform adjusted by implementing tokenization but initially failed to ensure irreversibility, as reverse engineering was possible with metadata.
process_and_outcome: CAC rectified issues without major fine, requiring enhanced anonymization protocols and third-party audits. Weibo published a compliance report.
source_rule: (4) "Anonymization" refers to the process in which personal information is handled so that it cannot be used to identify a specific natural person and cannot be restored after being so handled.
relevant_rules: Several Provisions on Internet Information Services

example_background: China Mobile Communications Group published guidelines in 2023 for handling telecom data, applying techniques such as generalization, suppression, and differential privacy to location and call records datasets used for network optimization. Over 1 billion user records were processed into research publications and shared with partners, ensuring no specific individual could be identified or data restored. This was part of state-owned enterprise efforts to align with national data laws.
process_and_outcome: CAC reviewed and approved the practices as exemplary. No penalties issued; framework adopted as industry reference.
source_rule: (4) "Anonymization" refers to the process in which personal information is handled so that it cannot be used to identify a specific natural person and cannot be restored after being so handled.
involved_parties: MIIT (Ministry of Industry and Information Technology)

example_name: Ping An Insurance Data Anonymization Practice
example_background: Ping An Group, a financial services giant, developed an anonymization pipeline for health and insurance claims data involving 300 million policyholders. Techniques included hashing, noise addition, and sampling to produce datasets for actuarial analysis and AI model training, verified through re-identification risk assessments showing less than 0.01% probability. Implemented since 2022 PIPL enforcement.
process_and_outcome: Third-party audits confirmed compliance; used in regulatory filings without issues. Recognized in industry reports.
source_rule: (4) "Anonymization" refers to the process in which personal information is handled so that it cannot be used to identify a specific natural person and cannot be restored after being so handled.
involved_parties: China Banking and Insurance Regulatory Commission (CBIRC)

example_background: Baidu Inc. established a data governance center in 2022 to process search queries, maps, and Apollo autonomous driving data. Anonymization involved irreversible transformations like temporal aggregation and location perturbation, applied to billions of records shared for public research, ensuring datasets could not link to natural persons.
process_and_outcome: CAC certified the framework during 2023 review; no enforcement actions. Baidu released open anonymized datasets.
source_rule: (4) "Anonymization" refers to the process in which personal information is handled so that it cannot be used to identify a specific natural person and cannot be restored after being so handled.

example_background: WeChat, operated by Tencent, faced examination over its handling of user data collected through its messaging and social platform serving hundreds of millions of users in China. The platform gathered extensive personal information including chat records, location data, and behavioral patterns. Concerns arose when data processing methods allowed re-identification of users when combined with publicly available information or cross-referenced datasets. This occurred amid broader scrutiny of internet platforms' data practices, where de-identified data pools were found to retain identifiable traces such as unique device fingerprints or sequential interaction patterns that could link back to specific individuals without substantial additional safeguards like robust anonymization techniques or data minimization.
process_and_outcome: The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) conducted a review in 2021, identifying shortcomings in data de-identification processes. Tencent was required to overhaul its data handling protocols, implement stricter anonymization measures, and submit compliance reports. No monetary penalty was publicly detailed, but operational adjustments were mandated to prevent re-identification risks.
source_rule: (3) "De-identification" refers to the process of handling personal information to make it impossible to identify a specific natural person without the help of additional information.
relevant_rules: Personal Information Protection Law (PIPL) of China

example_background: Baidu's suite of mobile applications, including search and mapping services, collected user data such as search queries, location coordinates, and device information. A 2022 government inspection found that Baidu's de-identification processes for aggregated data used in advertising and recommendations were insufficient. Retained elements like unique query sequences and IP-linked locations enabled potential re-identification when merged with other datasets, affecting millions of users' privacy in the context of algorithmic personalization.
process_and_outcome: CAC announced the findings in a joint ministry inspection, requiring Baidu to rectify practices within a specified period. Baidu implemented enhanced anonymization tools and faced administrative guidance without specified fines publicly.
source_rule: (3) "De-identification" refers to the process of handling personal information to make it impossible to identify a specific natural person without the help of additional information.
involved_parties: Ministry of Industry and Information Technology (MIIT)
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of De-identification Status

example_background: China Mobile, a state-owned telecommunications giant, processes enormous volumes of call detail records, SMS metadata, and location data from hundreds of millions of subscribers. In preparation for PIPL compliance, the company established a de-identification pipeline that applies perturbation, suppression, and synthetic data generation to render information non-attributable to specific individuals without additional data. This framework was tested across billing, network optimization, and marketing analytics operations.
process_and_outcome: Following a 2023 internal and regulatory audit, China Mobile's methods were validated as meeting de-identification criteria, allowing continued data use for secondary purposes. The company published whitepapers and trained staff, positioning it as a leader in telco data handling.
source_rule: (3) "De-identification" refers to the process of handling personal information to make it impossible to identify a specific natural person without the help of additional information.

example_background: ByteDance's platforms, including Douyin in China, handle video interaction data, user profiles, and biometric inputs from a global user base. A 2023 review identified gaps in de-identification for research and recommendation datasets, where pseudonymized IDs and behavioral timelines could be cross-referenced to re-identify users, particularly in content moderation and ad targeting workflows.
process_and_outcome: CAC mandated corrections following a special campaign on app data practices. ByteDance upgraded its privacy engineering, adding risk assessments for re-identification and halting non-compliant processing, with ongoing monitoring.
source_rule: (3) "De-identification" refers to the process of handling personal information to make it impossible to identify a specific natural person without the help of additional information.

example_background: JD.com, a leading e-commerce platform, rolled out an enterprise-wide de-identification system for customer purchase histories, logistics data, and preference profiles. Techniques included hashing, noise addition, and l-diversity to ensure datasets used in supply chain analytics and personalized shopping remained unlinkable to specific persons, even with external enhancements.
process_and_outcome: JD.com passed a third-party certification audit in 2022 and integrated the system into operations, demonstrating effective data utility preservation while adhering to standards, with no regulatory interventions.

Inspection revealed that anonymized datasets for market analysis still contained identifiable patterns like rare product combinations and regional delivery details that could reconstruct user identities with minimal extra information.
process_and_outcome: Part of a 2022 multi-agency app inspection, Suning was directed to strengthen de-identification controls and conduct self-assessments. Remediation was completed under supervision, avoiding further penalties.

example_background: Ping An Group, a financial services conglomerate, applied advanced de-identification protocols to health records, insurance claims, and financial transactions data serving tens of millions of policyholders. Methods encompassed secure multi-party computation, tokenization, and re-identification risk modeling to facilitate actuarial analysis and fraud detection without exposing individuals.
process_and_outcome: In 2023, Ping An's practices were endorsed in a regulatory filing, contributing to its selection as a model for fintech data handling. The framework supported innovation while maintaining compliance.

example_name: Beijing Zhiliao Technology Co., Ltd. Algorithmic Recommendation Fine
example_background: Beijing Zhiliao Technology Co., Ltd., operator of the 'Soul' social app, utilized automated systems to analyze user personal information including behavioral patterns, interests, and interaction data to generate personalized content recommendations and user matching services. The company's algorithms processed large volumes of user data collected through app interactions, profile inputs, and usage habits to automatically evaluate and decide on content feeds and social connections. During the period from 2021 to 2022, the platform amassed millions of users, primarily young adults, and the automated decision-making features were core to its business model, involving continuous data analysis for real-time adjustments to user experiences. The Cyberspace Administration of China (CAC) conducted inspections as part of broader efforts to regulate algorithmic applications in internet platforms.
process_and_outcome: The CAC determined that the company failed to conduct required assessments for automated decision-making and neglected to inform users about decision logic. A fine of RMB 2 million was imposed in August 2022, along with orders to rectify the practices and enhance data handling procedures.
source_rule: (2) “Automated decision-making“, refers to the use of computer programs to automatically analyze, evaluate, and make decisions on personal information on personal behavior habits, hobbies or economic, health, credit status, and so forth.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Administration of Algorithmic Recommendation in Internet Information Services
Personal Information Protection Law of China (PIPL)

example_name: Beijing ByteDance Shanghai Yintong Data Technology Co., Ltd. Penalty
example_background: Beijing ByteDance Shanghai Yintong Data Technology Co., Ltd., responsible for certain data operations linked to platforms like Toutiao, employed computer algorithms to automatically analyze user personal information such as reading habits, search histories, location data, and device usage to evaluate and deliver tailored news feeds, advertisements, and content recommendations. The system processed vast datasets from millions of daily active users, using machine learning models to infer user preferences and economic status indicators for precise targeting. This occurred amid China's 2021-2022 regulatory push on data protection, where the company handled sensitive personal behavioral data without fully transparent mechanisms.
process_and_outcome: Following CAC inspection, the company was found lacking in providing users with options to opt-out of personalized recommendations and failed to disclose algorithmic decision-making details. It received a RMB 500,000 fine in August 2022 and was required to implement compliance fixes.

This practice was scrutinized during regulatory audits in 2022 focusing on algorithmic uses in commercial services.
process_and_outcome: The CAC identified deficiencies in user notification regarding automated decisions and lack of appeal mechanisms. Alibaba was fined RMB 500,000 in August 2022 and instructed to revise its data processing protocols.

example_background: Tencent, through its WeChat platform, serves over 1 billion monthly active users in China and handles vast amounts of personal information including chat records, payment details, social connections, and location data. As the entity deciding the purposes (social networking, payments, mini-programs) and methods (storage, encryption, sharing) of this data processing, Tencent implemented features like facial recognition for payments and data analytics for content recommendation. In 2021-2022, investigations revealed that certain mini-programs within WeChat collected facial images, device information, and precise locations from users without explicit consent or necessity, exceeding declared purposes. This involved over 1.2 billion instances of unauthorized collection across 3,663 mini-programs, affecting user privacy in everyday interactions like shopping and gaming.
process_and_outcome: CAC announced penalties in August 2022, fining Tencent RMB 1 billion for violations in personal information handling. The regulator ordered immediate cessation of illegal practices, data deletion where applicable, and a six-month rectification plan. Tencent submitted compliance reports and enhanced its privacy policy disclosures.
source_rule: (1) “Personal information handlers“ refers to organizations or individuals that independently make decisions about the purposes and methods of personal information handling in personal information handling activities.
relevant_rules: Provisions on the Determination of and Handling of Illegal Collections and Use of Personal Information by Apps

example_background: Shanghai Qiming Xinghe Information Technology provided data aggregation services, collecting and processing personal information from public sources and apps, deciding purposes like marketing lists and credit assessments. The firm handled data of over 1 billion individuals, including phone numbers, addresses, and consumption habits, using web crawlers and APIs for methods of processing and resale to clients in finance and real estate.
process_and_outcome: CAC imposed a RMB 1.2 billion fine in August 2022 for illegal data trading and processing without consent. Operations were suspended, data assets confiscated, and the company dissolved. This was part of a crackdown on data intermediaries.

process_and_outcome: CAC fined JD.com RMB 1 billion in certain proceedings, requiring data minimization, enhanced notices, and audits. The company updated its app to comply with consent rules and reported progress quarterly.

example_background: Weibo, a microblogging platform similar to Twitter, handles personal information from over 500 million users, deciding on content moderation, advertising, and trend analysis methods. It processed real-name data, locations, and interaction histories, with incidents of excessive collection for anti-spam purposes extending to profiling without limits.
process_and_outcome: In 2022, CAC fined Weibo RMB 1.25 billion alongside others, mandating 6-month rectifications including data mapping and consent revamps. Weibo improved its privacy center and reduced data retention periods.
source_rule: (1) “Personal information handlers“ refers to organizations or individuals that independently make decisions about the purposes and methods of personal information handling in personal information handling activities.

example_background: China Telecom, a state-owned telecommunications giant, handles personal information of over 400 million mobile subscribers, deciding on billing, service provisioning, and network optimization methods. Following PIPL implementation, the company conducted enterprise-wide audits, implemented data classification systems, and appointed dedicated data protection officers to oversee processing activities across its vast infrastructure.
process_and_outcome: CAC recognized China Telecom's proactive compliance in 2022-2023 reviews, with no major penalties issued. The firm published annual transparency reports detailing data handling volumes, consent rates, and breach responses, serving as a model for state enterprises.
source_rule: (1) “Personal information handlers“ refers to organizations or individuals that independently make decisions about the purposes and methods of personal information handling in personal information handling activities.
